questions.scenario: A small team has no platform engineers, no Kubernetes tooling, and one containerized background worker that scales from queue depth. Which option is usually the smaller first fit?

options.text: Container Apps, because the team needs managed container execution more than a Kubernetes cluster.
AKS, because a worker is not real until it has a pod.
A public VM with SSH open so debugging is easier.
Azure DNS, because queues are networked.

questions.explanation: Container Apps can run event-scaled containers without making the team operate Kubernetes first.

questions.scenario: Which responsibilities remain with the team after choosing AKS?

options.text: Choosing node pool strategy and capacity.
Writing useful probes, resource requests, and deployment manifests.
Understanding ingress, services, workload identity, and logs.
Ignoring Kubernetes upgrades because Azure deletes that concern completely.

questions.explanation: AKS reduces control plane work, but cluster and workload operations still belong to the team.

questions.scenario: What is the cleanest AKS decision rule from the article?

options.text: Use AKS whenever a Dockerfile exists.
Use AKS when Kubernetes itself is the platform the organization intends to operate.
Use AKS to avoid learning about networking and identity.
Use AKS only for virtual machines that cannot run containers.

questions.explanation: AKS is the right answer when Kubernetes gives platform leverage. It is not the default answer for every container.
